name: Belize
tagline: From the great Maya cities of Caracol, Xunantunich and Lamanai, through the logwood and mahogany camps of the British Baymen, the slavery on which they rested and the Creole, Garifuna, Maya and Mestizo society that grew up around them, to the colony of British Honduras and independence as Belize on 21 September 1981 — its frontier still claimed by Guatemala before the International Court of Justice.
summary: Belize is a small, English-speaking nation on the Caribbean coast of Central America, the only country in the region with a British colonial past rather than a Spanish one. Its lowland forests were once dense with Maya settlement — Caracol, Xunantunich, Lamanai and Altun Ha among them — before the Classic Maya world unravelled in the ninth and tenth centuries. From the seventeenth century British buccaneers and woodcutters, the "Baymen", settled the coast to cut logwood and then mahogany, importing enslaved Africans for the work and repelling a final Spanish assault at the Battle of St. George's Caye in 1798; the colony of British Honduras was formally constituted in 1862. A distinctive plural society of Creoles, Garifuna, Maya and Mestizos took shape, and under George Cadle Price and the People's United Party the colony moved through self-government to independence as Belize on 21 September 1981 — though Guatemala's long-standing claim to the territory, neutrally before the International Court of Justice since 2019, remains unresolved.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: On 21 September 1981 the British colony of British Honduras — renamed Belize in June 1973 — became the independent nation of Belize, the last British territory on the American mainland to win statehood. Independence came after three decades of nationalist politics led by George Cadle Price and the People's United Party, which had carried the colony through universal adult suffrage in 1954, internal self-government in 1964, and the renaming of 1973. It was achieved within the Commonwealth, with the British monarch as head of state represented by a governor-general, and Price as the new country's first prime minister. The path had been complicated by Guatemala's territorial claim: Guatemala refused to recognise the new state, and a British garrison remained in Belize for years after independence to deter any incursion. The United Nations General Assembly had repeatedly backed Belize's right to self-determination, and dozens of nations recognised it immediately; the United States did so on 29 October 1981. Belize thus entered the world as a sovereign state with its borders intact but its sovereignty still formally contested by its western neighbour.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: 21 September 1981, from the United Kingdom — the last British possession on the Central American mainland to gain independence, within the Commonwealth

quickFacts.label: Former name
quickFacts.value: British Honduras; the colony was renamed Belize in June 1973, eight years before independence

quickFacts.label: Father of the Nation
quickFacts.value: George Cadle Price (1919–2011), leader of the People's United Party and first prime minister

quickFacts.label: Colonial founding
quickFacts.value: Constituted the colony of British Honduras in 1862, governed under Jamaica; a separate Crown Colony from 1871, independent of Jamaica from 1884

quickFacts.label: Guatemalan claim
quickFacts.value: A long-running territorial claim, now before the International Court of Justice after referenda in Guatemala (2018) and Belize (2019)

quickFacts.label: World Heritage
quickFacts.value: The Belize Barrier Reef Reserve System — the largest reef in the Atlantic-Caribbean and second-largest on Earth — inscribed by UNESCO in 1996

eras.title: The land of the Maya
eras.standfirst: Long before any European arrived, the lowlands of Belize were dense with Maya cities — Caracol, Xunantunich, Lamanai and Altun Ha — before the Classic Maya world came apart in the ninth and tenth centuries.
body: The forests and river valleys of what is now Belize lay at the heart of the Maya lowlands, settled by Maya-speaking peoples from at least the second millennium BCE. Over the Preclassic and Classic periods (roughly 2000 BCE to 900 CE) the region filled with farming villages and, in time, monumental cities: Caracol in the Maya Mountains grew into one of the largest urban centres of the southern lowlands, while Xunantunich, Lamanai, Altun Ha, Cahal Pech and Lubaantun ranged across the country. These were sophisticated societies, with achievements in agriculture, hieroglyphic writing, mathematics, astronomy and stone architecture, knit into the wider Maya world by trade, alliance and war.

Caracol shows how consequential these cities could be. In the sixth century it emerged as a great power of the lowlands, and its monuments record a celebrated victory: in 562 CE Caracol, allied with the rival superpower Calakmul, defeated the mighty city of Tikal across the modern Guatemalan border, after which Tikal carved no monuments for well over a century. At its height Caracol covered an enormous area and supported a population in the tens of thousands, sustained by terraced fields and a network of causeways — a forest metropolis rather than a village. Xunantunich, with its towering pyramid "El Castillo", and Lamanai, set on a lagoon, were likewise major ceremonial and political centres.

From the late eighth century the Classic Maya order began to fracture. Across the southern lowlands one city after another stopped raising monuments and was gradually abandoned, in a drawn-out "collapse" that archaeologists attribute not to a single calamity but to a tangle of causes — prolonged drought, environmental stress, overpopulation and endemic warfare — varying from place to place. Caracol and Xunantunich faded; yet the collapse was uneven. Lamanai, exceptionally, remained continuously occupied for some three millennia and was still a living Maya town when the Spanish arrived, and Maya communities persisted across the region. The great cities fell silent, but the Maya themselves did not disappear, and their descendants remain part of Belize today.

events.title: Maya settlement of the lowlands
events.summary: Maya-speaking peoples settled the lowland forests and river valleys of present-day Belize, founding farming villages that over centuries grew into towns and cities across the heart of the Maya world.

events.title: Caracol defeats Tikal
events.summary: Caracol, allied with Calakmul, won a decisive "Star War" victory over Tikal in 562 CE; the defeated city carved no monuments for over a century, marking Caracol's rise as a great power of the southern lowlands.

events.title: Height of the Classic Maya cities
events.summary: Caracol, Xunantunich, Lamanai, Altun Ha and others flourished as ceremonial and political centres, with monumental architecture, hieroglyphic inscriptions and populations in the tens of thousands sustained by intensive agriculture.

events.title: The Classic Maya collapse
events.summary: From the late eighth century the southern lowland cities ceased raising monuments and were abandoned, in a prolonged collapse attributed to drought, environmental stress and warfare — though Lamanai and scattered Maya communities endured.

pullquote.text: When Caracol star-warred Tikal in 562, the great city across the border fell silent for more than a hundred years — a measure of how powerful the Maya of Belize once were.

eras.title: The Baymen, the forest and the slaves
eras.standfirst: From the seventeenth century British buccaneers and woodcutters — the Baymen — settled the coast to cut logwood and mahogany, importing enslaved Africans for the work and beating off a final Spanish assault at St. George's Caye in 1798.
body: Spain claimed the whole Central American coast but never effectively settled the swampy, reef-guarded shore of what is now Belize. Into this gap came English and Scottish buccaneers and, from the mid-seventeenth century, woodcutters drawn by logwood, a small tree whose heartwood yielded a prized dye for Europe's textile mills. These settlers — who came to call themselves the Baymen, after the Bay of Honduras — established camps along the rivers and coast, governing themselves loosely without a formal colonial charter. As demand shifted in the later eighteenth century from logwood to the far more valuable mahogany, the scale of the cutting grew, and with it the settlement's need for land, capital and labour.

That labour was enslaved. From the early eighteenth century the Baymen imported enslaved Africans — many bought through Jamaica and Bermuda — to fell, square and haul timber out of the forest. Slavery in the settlement was shaped by the seasonal, dispersed work of logging rather than the gang labour of plantations, but it was brutal and resisted, with escapes and revolts. The treaties of Paris (1763) and Versailles (1783) had Spain concede the British the right to cut logwood, and after 1786 mahogany, within defined limits while reserving sovereignty — an ambiguous arrangement that left the settlement neither fully Spanish nor formally British, and would echo into later disputes over the territory's status.

Spain made repeated attempts to expel the settlers across the eighteenth century. The last and most famous came in 1798, when a Spanish fleet and troops from the Yucatán sailed to drive the Baymen out. In a series of engagements off St. George's Caye between 3 and 10 September 1798, the outnumbered settlers — woodcutters, a small British naval force and the enslaved Africans who fought alongside them — repelled the Spanish, who withdrew on 10 September. The Battle of St. George's Caye entered Belizean memory as a founding national legend and remains a public holiday; in practical terms it ended Spain's effort to dislodge the settlement and confirmed British predominance on the coast.

events.title: British woodcutters settle the coast
events.summary: English and Scottish buccaneers and logwood cutters began settling the coast of present-day Belize, beyond effective Spanish control; calling themselves the Baymen, they cut logwood for the European dye trade.

events.title: Enslaved Africans imported for the timber trade
events.summary: From the early 1700s the Baymen imported enslaved Africans, many via Jamaica and Bermuda, to cut and haul logwood and then mahogany — the labour on which the settlement's forest economy rested.

events.title: Spain concedes cutting rights
events.summary: By the treaties of Paris (1763) and Versailles (1783) Spain granted British subjects the right to cut logwood — and after 1786 the more valuable mahogany — within defined limits, while reserving sovereignty over the territory.

events.title: Battle of St. George's Caye
events.summary: In engagements off St. George's Caye, the outnumbered Baymen, a small British force and enslaved Africans repelled a Spanish fleet from the Yucatán; the Spanish withdrew on 10 September, ending the last attempt to expel the settlers.

pullquote.text: Neither fully Spanish nor formally British, the settlement of the Baymen rested on the dye trees of the forest and the labour of the enslaved Africans who cut them.
pullquote.attribution: The Bay settlement before British Honduras

eras.title: British Honduras and a plural society
eras.standfirst: The settlement was formally constituted the colony of British Honduras in 1862; emancipation, the arrival of the Garifuna and successive refugees forged a plural Creole, Garifuna, Maya and Mestizo society — and a frontier Guatemala would claim.
body: After 1798 the settlement consolidated under growing British control. Slavery was abolished across the British Empire from 1834, with a transitional "apprenticeship" ending in British Honduras on 1 August 1838; emancipation reshaped a society in which freed Africans and their descendants, increasingly known as Creoles, formed the core population of Belize Town and the timber camps. In 1859 Britain and Guatemala signed a boundary treaty fixing the limits of the settlement from the Río Hondo to the Sarstoon — an agreement whose disputed Article 7, calling for a road or other means of communication that was never built, would later become the seed of Guatemala's territorial claim.

On 12 May 1862 the settlement was formally declared the Colony of British Honduras, under a lieutenant-governor subordinate to the governor of Jamaica. It became a separate Crown Colony in 1871, with its self-governing legislative assembly replaced by an appointed council, and in 1884 it was finally separated from Jamaica under its own governor. The colony's economy remained narrowly tied to forest products — mahogany, logwood, chicle for chewing gum — leaving it vulnerable to the swings of distant markets, and its small white merchant elite governed a population of very different origins.

That population grew strikingly plural. The Garifuna (Garinagu) — a people of mixed African and Carib descent deported by the British from St. Vincent and the Bay Islands — settled the southern coast around Stann Creek and Punta Gorda from 1802, an arrival commemorated on 19 November as Garifuna Settlement Day. The Caste War of Yucatán from 1847 drove thousands of Maya and Spanish-speaking Mestizo refugees south into the colony's north and west, while later came East Indian and Chinese indentured labourers and Mennonite farming communities. By the early twentieth century British Honduras was a layered society of Creole, Garifuna, Maya, Mestizo and immigrant peoples — the foundation of modern Belize's distinctive Caribbean-Central American identity.

events.title: The Garifuna reach the southern coast
events.summary: The Garifuna (Garinagu), deported by the British from St. Vincent, settled the southern coast around Stann Creek and Punta Gorda; their arrival on 19 November is marked as Garifuna Settlement Day, a national holiday from 1941.

events.title: Emancipation completed
events.summary: Following abolition across the British Empire from 1834, the apprenticeship system ended in British Honduras on 1 August 1838, freeing the enslaved; the freed African population formed the core of the colony's Creole society.

events.title: Caste War refugees swell the population
events.summary: The Caste War of Yucatán drove thousands of Maya and Spanish-speaking Mestizo refugees into the colony's north and west, adding to the Creole and Garifuna populations and deepening Belize's plural society.

events.title: The Anglo-Guatemalan boundary treaty
events.summary: Britain and Guatemala signed a treaty fixing the settlement's limits from the Río Hondo to the Sarstoon; the unfulfilled Article 7, promising a means of communication never built, became the root of Guatemala's later claim.

events.title: The Colony of British Honduras
events.summary: The settlement was formally declared the Colony of British Honduras under a lieutenant-governor subordinate to Jamaica; it became a separate Crown Colony in 1871 and was made independent of Jamaica under its own governor in 1884.

pullquote.text: Creole, Garifuna, Maya, Mestizo and migrant — out of forest camps, emancipation and waves of refugees, British Honduras became one of the most plural societies in the Americas.

eras.title: Price, the PUP and independence
eras.standfirst: George Cadle Price and the People's United Party led British Honduras from universal suffrage through self-government and the renaming to Belize, to independence within the Commonwealth on 21 September 1981.
body: Modern Belizean nationalism crystallised after the Second World War. The devaluation of the colony's dollar in 1949 stoked popular anger, and on 29 September 1950 the People's United Party (PUP) was founded, soon led by George Cadle Price — a Belize City man of Creole and Mestizo descent, once destined for the priesthood, who would dominate the country's politics for a generation. The PUP campaigned for democratic rights and self-rule and won the elections held under universal adult suffrage, granted in 1954. Price became First Minister in 1961 and premier when Britain conceded internal self-government in 1964 under a new constitution, leaving only defence and foreign affairs in London's hands.

The advance to full independence was slowed above all by Guatemala, which claimed the whole territory and at times threatened to take it by force; the prospect of an unresolved border deterred Britain and worried Belizeans about defence. Price's government worked the diplomatic ground patiently, winning successive United Nations General Assembly resolutions affirming Belize's right to self-determination and territorial integrity and building support across the Caribbean, Latin America and the Non-Aligned Movement. In June 1973, as a marker of national identity, the colony's name was changed from British Honduras to Belize — the name long used for its principal river and town, eight years before sovereignty itself arrived.

Independence finally came on 21 September 1981, with Belize entering the Commonwealth as a sovereign state and George Price as its first prime minister. Because Guatemala still refused to recognise the new nation and renounced none of its claim, a British military garrison remained in Belize after independence to guarantee its security. The achievement nonetheless completed a remarkable arc: the last British colony on the Central American mainland had become an independent country, its borders intact and its multi-ethnic society self-governing, even as one fundamental question — its neighbour's claim — was carried unresolved into the independent era.

events.title: The People's United Party founded
events.summary: Amid anger over the 1949 currency devaluation, the People's United Party was founded and soon led by George Cadle Price; it championed democratic rights and self-rule and would dominate Belizean politics for decades.

events.title: Universal adult suffrage
events.summary: Britain granted universal adult suffrage to British Honduras in 1954; the PUP won the ensuing elections, beginning a long run of electoral dominance that carried Price's party toward self-government and independence.

events.title: Internal self-government
events.summary: Under a new constitution Britain granted internal self-government, retaining only defence and foreign affairs; George Price became premier, with Belizeans now running their own domestic affairs.

events.title: British Honduras renamed Belize
events.summary: In June 1973 the colony's name was officially changed from British Honduras to Belize, an assertion of national identity adopted eight years before formal independence.

events.title: Independence as Belize
events.summary: Belize became an independent state within the Commonwealth on 21 September 1981 with George Price as first prime minister — the last British colony on the Central American mainland to win independence, its frontier still claimed by Guatemala.

figures.name: George Cadle Price
figures.role: Father of the Nation; first Prime Minister of Belize
figures.blurb: Belize City man of Creole and Mestizo descent and a Roman Catholic intellectual once bound for the priesthood, Price led the People's United Party from the 1950s, served as First Minister and premier under British rule, and became independent Belize's first prime minister in 1981. Honoured as "Father of the Nation", he steered the country through suffrage, self-government and independence while defending its territory against Guatemala's claim.

pullquote.text: In June 1973 the colony shed the name British Honduras for Belize — claiming the nation in name eight years before it claimed it in law.

eras.title: Independent Belize and the Guatemalan question
eras.standfirst: Since independence Belize has been a stable Commonwealth democracy of two alternating parties, a reef-and-rainforest tourism economy and a famed barrier reef — while the unresolved Guatemalan claim has been carried, neutrally, before the International Court of Justice.
body: Independent Belize settled into one of the more stable democracies of the region, with power alternating peacefully between the People's United Party and the United Democratic Party. George Price lost office for the first time in 1984, returned in 1989, and lost again in 1993; later prime ministers — Manuel Esquivel, Said Musa, Dean Barrow and, from 2020, John Briceño — succeeded one another through regular elections. The economy diversified away from its old dependence on mahogany and chicle toward sugar, citrus, bananas, and increasingly tourism, built on the country's Maya ruins, rainforests and, above all, its coastline. The British garrison was eventually drawn down as relations stabilised, leaving Belize responsible for its own defence within the Commonwealth.

Belize's greatest natural asset gained world recognition in 1996, when UNESCO inscribed the Belize Barrier Reef Reserve System as a World Heritage site. Comprising seven protected areas — among them the Blue Hole, Half Moon Caye and Glover's Reef — it is the largest reef complex in the Atlantic-Caribbean region and the second-largest reef system on Earth, harbouring manatees, marine turtles and the American crocodile. Charles Darwin had called it the most remarkable reef in the West Indies. The reef underpins both tourism and fisheries, and its conservation — after a period on UNESCO's danger list owing to coastal development and offshore drilling threats, from which it was removed in 2018 — has become central to Belize's economy and identity.

The defining unresolved issue remains Guatemala's claim to Belizean territory, which both governments have committed to settle by law rather than force. Under a Special Agreement signed in 2008, the dispute was put to referenda: Guatemalans voted to refer it to the International Court of Justice on 15 April 2018, and Belizeans did the same on 8 May 2019. The Court was formally seised of the case — "Guatemala's Territorial, Insular and Maritime Claim (Guatemala/Belize)" — on 7 June 2019, and the parties have since filed written pleadings ahead of a final, binding judgment. Belize approaches the proceedings as a defence of its long-recognised borders and Guatemala as the pursuit of a historic claim; the matter sits, neutrally, with the world court, awaiting resolution as Belize marks more than four decades of independence.

events.title: Barrier Reef made a World Heritage site
events.summary: UNESCO inscribed the Belize Barrier Reef Reserve System — seven protected areas including the Blue Hole and Half Moon Caye — as a World Heritage site, recognising the largest reef in the Atlantic-Caribbean and second-largest on Earth.

events.title: Special Agreement on the dispute
events.summary: Belize and Guatemala signed a Special Agreement committing to submit Guatemala's territorial, insular and maritime claim to the International Court of Justice, subject to approval by referendum in each country.

events.title: Belize votes to go to the ICJ
events.summary: After Guatemalans approved referral on 15 April 2018, Belizeans voted on 8 May 2019 to submit the dispute to the International Court of Justice; the Court was seised of the case on 7 June 2019.

events.title: Pleadings before the World Court
events.summary: Guatemala and Belize filed written pleadings before the International Court of Justice in the case "Guatemala's Territorial, Insular and Maritime Claim (Guatemala/Belize)", moving toward a final, binding judgment on the century-old dispute.

figures.name: Dean Barrow
figures.blurb: Belize's first prime minister of African Belizean descent and its longest-serving, leading the United Democratic Party through three terms; under his government the Special Agreement with Guatemala advanced toward referenda and the International Court of Justice.

figures.name: John Briceño
figures.blurb: Leader of the People's United Party who became prime minister in 2020, governing as Belize pressed its case at the International Court of Justice and managed the reef-and-tourism economy that anchors the modern nation.

pullquote.text: Belize and Guatemala chose the courtroom over the frontier: a century-old claim now rests, neutrally, with the World Court in The Hague.
pullquote.attribution: Belize and the International Court of Justice

figures.name: George Cadle Price
figures.role: Father of the Nation; first Prime Minister of Belize
figures.blurb: Leader of the People's United Party from the 1950s who carried British Honduras through universal suffrage, self-government and the renaming to Belize, becoming independent Belize's first prime minister in 1981 and its most revered statesman.

figures.name: Caracol (Maya city)
figures.role: Great Classic Maya city of the southern lowlands
figures.blurb: One of the largest Maya urban centres of the lowlands, in the Maya Mountains of western Belize, famed for its 562 CE "Star War" victory over Tikal and its vast network of terraces and causeways supporting tens of thousands of people.

figures.name: Dean Barrow
figures.blurb: Belize's longest-serving prime minister and first of African Belizean descent, who led the United Democratic Party and oversaw the advance of the Guatemalan dispute toward the International Court of Justice.
